items.q: Does a catalyst change the position of equilibrium?
items.correct: No — it speeds up the forward and reverse reactions equally, so equilibrium is reached faster but the position and yield are unchanged.
items.misconception: A catalyst shifts the equilibrium towards the products, increasing the yield.
items.random_wrong: A catalyst always shifts the equilibrium in the exothermic direction.
items.why: Students conflate 'faster' with 'more product'; a catalyst lowers the activation energy of both directions by the same amount.

items.q: Is energy released or absorbed when bonds break?
items.correct: Breaking bonds always absorbs energy; energy is released when new bonds form, and the balance determines the sign of ΔH.
items.misconception: Breaking bonds releases energy, which is why fuels give out heat.
items.random_wrong: Bond breaking neither absorbs nor releases energy.
items.why: Everyday language about 'energy stored in bonds' makes students think breaking them sets energy free; combustion is exothermic because the new bonds in CO₂ and H₂O are stronger.

items.q: Why does a small temperature rise increase reaction rate so sharply?
items.correct: Mainly because many more molecules have energy above the activation energy, not just because collisions are slightly more frequent.
items.misconception: Higher temperature increases the rate mainly because the particles collide more often.
items.random_wrong: Higher temperature increases the rate because it lowers the activation energy.
items.why: The collision-frequency increase is small; the exponential growth of the high-energy tail of the Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution dominates, which students rarely appreciate.

items.q: How many moles of molecules are in 8.0 g of O₂ (Mr = 32.0)?
items.why: It's 0.25 mol. “0.50 mol” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: ΔH negative. “ΔH positive” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A catalyst increases the rate of a reaction by:
items.correct: Providing an alternative route with lower activation energy
items.misconception: Giving the particles more kinetic energy
items.random_wrong: Shifting the equilibrium towards the products
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Providing an alternative route with lower activation energy. “Giving the particles more kinetic energy” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: For N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃ (forward reaction exothermic), increasing the temperature shifts the equilibrium:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: To the left. “To the right” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Calculate the heat released when 50.0 g of water rises by 10.0 °C (c = 4.18 J g⁻¹ K⁻¹). Give q in joules.
items.why: Exact answer: 2090 J. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
